caveats: a: OEWS does not publish a six-digit 721191 occupation mix and excludes self-employed owner labor.
a: NAICS 721100 includes hotels and casino-related employment patterns that differ materially from small inns.
a: Task exposure is a judgmental mapping, not an observed automation share.
rho: The principal adoption study covers hotel chains globally, not single-property U.S. inns.
rho: Survey expectations for full automation are intentions, not realized deployments.
rho: Small inns may adopt inexpensive tools quickly but lack integration staff and clean operating data.
e: The frozen firm count is itself an estimate bridged from receipts and a sector margin.
e: B&B businesses often bundle operating goodwill with owner-occupied real estate.
e: No source measures eligibility among the nine frozen LMM firms directly.
s5: Gallup measures plans, not completed transactions, and spans all industries and firm sizes.
s5: BizBuySell does not disclose the at-risk B&B population or the number of underlying observations on the benchmark page.
s5: The observed BizBuySell firms have median revenue far below the frozen LMM earnings-band population.
q: No source directly observes benefit retention after AI adoption in B&B inns.
q: Local lodging markets vary sharply in seasonality, supply constraints, and price competition.
q: OTA fees affect distribution economics, while q concerns retention of the implemented benefit rather than the gross room rate.
d5: The BLS projection is for NAICS 721 and reports real output, not B&B room-night quantity.
d5: The STR forecast is for U.S. hotels and covers only the near term.
d5: B&B demand is concentrated in leisure destinations and can be more seasonal and locally idiosyncratic than national lodging demand.
o: The estimate distinguishes task automation from elimination of the accountable property operator.
o: Some jurisdictions and guests may accept remote management, but physical service delivery still requires accountable coordination.
o: A property that removes personalized service and breakfast may migrate outside NAICS 721191 rather than represent software-only supply of the same basket.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Guests buy short stays, usually at a property-set room rate that includes breakfast; bookings arrive directly or through online travel agencies that use commission or net-rate models. Revenue repeats with room-night demand but is not generally contractual or subscription-based.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure real-estate holdings, residences without an operating lodging business, informal short-term rentals lacking the bed-and-breakfast service bundle, captive lodging, non-control financing vehicles, and establishments classified as hotels, motels, casino hotels, hostels, or vacation cabins.
lens.included_activities: U.S. bed-and-breakfast inn operators providing short-term guest lodging, a full breakfast, personalized service, reservation handling, housekeeping, and related guest support to external travelers.

a.bridge: S2 measures the broader NAICS 721100 traveler-accommodation occupation mix rather than NAICS 721191, while S3 through S5 describe occupation tasks rather than AI exposure. I mapped reservation, records, billing, routine guest messaging, marketing, scheduling, and revenue-analysis tasks to high exposure; mapped cleaning, food preparation, maintenance, and in-person exception handling to low exposure; then judged the wage-weighted share using S2 mean wages. Large hotels and casino hotels in S2 likely have more specialized clerical and management staff than bed-and-breakfast inns, biasing the proxy upward, while unreported owner labor at small inns biases it downward.
caveats: OEWS does not publish a six-digit 721191 occupation mix and excludes self-employed owner labor.
NAICS 721100 includes hotels and casino-related employment patterns that differ materially from small inns.
Task exposure is a judgmental mapping, not an observed automation share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit NAICS 721191 occupation-by-wage table that includes proprietor hours, combined with time-and-motion interviews from at least 20 LMM inn operators allocating hours across reservations, guest messaging, revenue management, marketing, bookkeeping, breakfast, housekeeping, maintenance, and exception handling.
a.rationale: The broader traveler-accommodation mix is dominated by physical work: building and grounds cleaning is 26.76% of employment and food preparation and serving is 23.74%, while office and administrative support is 18.70% (S2). Desk clerks alone are 13.18%, and their O*NET tasks include reservations, account records, charges, messages, inquiries, bookkeeping, and scheduling (S3), all substantially exposed to language and workflow automation. Housekeeping's core duties remain physical (S4). The range therefore allows meaningful exposure in front-office and owner-manager work without treating room cleaning, breakfast service, or facilities work as substitutable by current AI.

d5.bridge: S11 projects real output for the broad accommodation subsector from 2024 to 2034, while S10 forecasts near-term U.S. hotel room demand, neither isolating bed-and-breakfast inns or exactly measuring constant-quality room-night quantity. I combine the long-run real-output direction with the near-term demand slowdown and set a more conservative five-year ratio for B&Bs because alternative accommodations compete strongly for leisure travelers. Real-output growth can overstate quantity if quality improves; hotel forecasts can understate B&B resilience in experience-led destinations.
caveats: The BLS projection is for NAICS 721 and reports real output, not B&B room-night quantity.
The STR forecast is for U.S. hotels and covers only the near term.
B&B demand is concentrated in leisure destinations and can be more seasonal and locally idiosyncratic than national lodging demand.
d5.change_evidence: STR or AirDNA room-night and occupancy histories and forecasts specifically for independent inns and B&Bs, separated from hotels and whole-home rentals, with a constant-quality adjustment and destination-level supply controls through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS projects accommodation real output to grow at a 2.9% compound annual rate from 2024 through 2034 (S11). The nearer-term STR and Tourism Economics forecast is softer: U.S. lodging demand fell 0.5% in 2025 and was expected to rise 0.4% in 2026 amid economic and international-travel headwinds (S10). A modestly positive five-year base balances those signals while allowing recession, substitution toward short-term rentals, or stronger experience-led leisure demand in the bounds.

caveats: The frozen firm count is itself an estimate bridged from receipts and a sector margin.
B&B businesses often bundle operating goodwill with owner-occupied real estate.
No source measures eligibility among the nine frozen LMM firms directly.
e.change_evidence: Property-level financials and operating profiles for all firms in the frozen LMM count, including room revenue share, event revenue, employee count, owner hours, property ownership, related-party arrangements, licenses, and whether operations can continue under hired management.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition requires short-term lodging, personalized service, and a full breakfast included in the room rate (S1), so active firms clearly serve external customers through repeat room-night transactions. At the frozen LMM earnings band, firms should generally be substantial operating businesses rather than hobby properties. I exclude the minority whose economics are chiefly owner-occupied real estate, whose service operation cannot be separated from the proprietor, or whose revenue mix no longer matches the industry. BizBuySell's observed B&B sales confirm that operating businesses can transfer, but its typical sold firms are much smaller than the frozen earnings band and often include real estate (S8).

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 721191 ($447,049,910) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 721191 ($1,273,857,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2445 (margins.json[721]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hotel/Gaming EBITDA/Sales 24.45%); revenue/employee $104,922 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 721191); class <5: 1,685 firms, avg revenue/firm $264,401 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,646 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 401 firms, avg revenue/firm $610,204 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $149,195 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 205 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,108,834 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $271,110 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 125 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,196,888 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $537,139 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,763,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,898,054 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,965,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $725,004 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 9

caveats: The estimate distinguishes task automation from elimination of the accountable property operator.
Some jurisdictions and guests may accept remote management, but physical service delivery still requires accountable coordination.
A property that removes personalized service and breakfast may migrate outside NAICS 721191 rather than represent software-only supply of the same basket.
o.change_evidence: Guest-level operating data from technology-forward inns comparing fully staffed and self-service properties on labor presence, regulatory requirements, breakfast delivery, cleaning, incidents, complaint escalation, guest satisfaction, repeat bookings, and whether the property remains classified as a B&B.
o.rationale: The current service basket is inherently physical: the NAICS definition requires short-term lodging in a private home or converted small building, personalized service, and a full breakfast (S1). O*NET shows housekeeping requires room cleaning, linen movement, replenishment, waste removal, and property protection (S4), while lodging managers remain accountable for complaints, facilities, staff, cleanliness, payments, procurement, and outside services (S5). Digital check-in and automated communications can self-serve part of the stay, but software cannot supply the room, breakfast, sanitation, safety response, or property accountability. Only conversion toward minimally serviced rentals or elimination of the B&B bundle drives the lower bound.

caveats: No source directly observes benefit retention after AI adoption in B&B inns.
Local lodging markets vary sharply in seasonality, supply constraints, and price competition.
OTA fees affect distribution economics, while q concerns retention of the implemented benefit rather than the gross room rate.
q.change_evidence: Property-level monthly panels from independent inns showing direct and OTA channel mix, room rates, occupancy, commissions, payroll savings after automation, promotional discounting, guest-acquisition cost, and contribution margin for at least three years after implementation, paired with local competitor pricing.
q.rationale: Room nights are sold at property-set prices rather than under cost-plus contracts, and Booking.com's commission model explicitly leaves price setting with the property while the platform retains a percentage of booking value (S9). That structure creates no automatic contractual pass-through of lower labor cost. Over five years, however, guests compare close substitutes, online travel agencies intermediate demand, and competitors can reinvest savings into price, amenities, or service. I therefore expect the operator to retain most, but not all, of an implemented gross benefit on a discounted basis.

rho.bridge: S6 surveys global hotel chains about current integration, staffing effects, and expected function-level automation, and S12 surveys hospitality leaders across more than 13,100 venues, not U.S. LMM bed-and-breakfast firms. I translate their adoption signals into the five-year implemented share of the exposed opportunity, discounting for small-operator budgets, fragmented property systems, the industry's personalized-service promise, and the need for human exception handling. Chain respondents probably have more integration resources than independent inns, biasing implementation upward; simpler inn workflows may partially offset that bias.
caveats: The principal adoption study covers hotel chains globally, not single-property U.S. inns.
Survey expectations for full automation are intentions, not realized deployments.
Small inns may adopt inexpensive tools quickly but lack integration staff and clean operating data.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of independent U.S. inns using integrated property-management, channel-management, voice, messaging, and bookkeeping agents, reporting baseline and two-year labor hours by workflow, exception rates, guest satisfaction, and realized headcount avoidance.
rho.rationale: In S6, 35% reported some AI features integrated, 25% reported pilots, and 27% used public tools, while only 1% said AI was central to the business model. Respondents expected reservations and call centers, customer data, revenue management, and digital marketing to be the functions most likely to be fully automated within five years, but 26% expected widespread full automation to remain out of reach. S12 likewise found strong intent but limited readiness and budget pressure. This supports partial implementation centered on communications, reservations, marketing, pricing support, and back-office workflows rather than unattended operation.

s5.bridge: S7 measures stated five-year plans among U.S. business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM bed-and-breakfast firms. I use the 22% employer-owner sell-or-transfer plan rate as an anchor, reduce for plans that fail or become closures or non-control family reorganizations, and offset that reduction because lodging assets have an observable B&B sale market in S8 and owner age is high across U.S. employer firms. The resulting proxy could be high if proprietor dependence makes LMM inns hard to sell, or low if aging inn owners transact faster than employer owners generally.
caveats: Gallup measures plans, not completed transactions, and spans all industries and firm sizes.
BizBuySell does not disclose the at-risk B&B population or the number of underlying observations on the benchmark page.
The observed BizBuySell firms have median revenue far below the frozen LMM earnings-band population.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of LMM U.S. inn transactions from CoStar, Real Capital Analytics, Axial, SBA 7(a) loan records, and hospitality brokers, matched to the beginning-of-period population and coded for control sale, family transfer, internal reorganization, closure, and continued ownership.
s5.rationale: Gallup found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older (S7). BizBuySell reports completed B&B sales from 2021 through 2025, a median 302 days on market, and a 0.78 average sale-to-ask ratio, demonstrating a functioning but not especially liquid transfer channel (S8). Because intentions exceed completed transfers and qualifying control is narrower than any transfer, the base does not exceed the employer-owner planning anchor.

principal_driver: Automatable reservation, communication, pricing-support, marketing, and back-office work can reduce the administrative burden of a labor-intensive lodging operation.
principal_weakness: Only nine firms are estimated in-band, and no source directly measures qualifying five-year control transfers or AI labor outcomes for LMM U.S. B&B inns.
